passages.explanation: On a Sabbath Jesus is going through the grainfields, and his disciples pick heads of grain, rub them in their hands, and eat the kernels. Some Pharisees challenge them: why are you doing what is unlawful on the Sabbath? Jesus responds by citing David, who entered the house of God and ate the consecrated bread — which only priests were allowed to eat — and gave some to his companions. Then he declares: the Son of Man is Lord of the Sabbath. Jesus doesn't argue that the Sabbath doesn't matter. He claims authority over it. The one who made the Sabbath gets to define what it means.

On another Sabbath Jesus enters the synagogue and teaches. A man with a shrivelled right hand is there. The Pharisees and teachers of the law watch closely to see if Jesus will heal on the Sabbath, looking for a reason to accuse him. Jesus knows their thoughts. He tells the man to stand in front of everyone. Then he asks: which is lawful on the Sabbath — to do good or to do evil, to save life or to destroy it? He looks around at them all and tells the man: stretch out your hand. He does, and his hand is completely restored. The Pharisees are furious and begin to discuss what they might do to Jesus. Jesus forces the question into the open — he heals publicly, deliberately, and dares them to argue that leaving a man suffering is more righteous than healing him.

Jesus goes out to a mountainside to pray and spends the whole night praying to God. When morning comes, he calls his disciples and chooses twelve of them, whom he designates apostles: Simon (whom he names Peter), Andrew his brother, James, John, Philip, Bartholomew, Matthew, Thomas, James son of Alphaeus, Simon called the Zealot, Judas son of James, and Judas Iscariot who becomes a traitor. The selection happens after an all-night prayer session — this isn't a casual decision. The twelve represent a reconstituted Israel (twelve tribes), and the group is deliberately diverse: fishermen, a tax collector, a political revolutionary.

Jesus comes down with them and stands on a level place — Luke's version of the Sermon on the Mount is the Sermon on the Plain. He looks at his disciples and says: blessed are you who are poor, for yours is the kingdom of God. Blessed are you who hunger now, for you will be satisfied. Blessed are you who weep now, for you will laugh. Blessed are you when people hate you, exclude you, insult you, and reject your name as evil because of the Son of Man — rejoice and leap for joy, because great is your reward in heaven. Then the woes: woe to you who are rich, for you've already received your comfort. Woe to you who are well fed now, for you will go hungry. Woe to you who laugh now, for you will mourn and weep. Woe to you when everyone speaks well of you — that's how their ancestors treated the false prophets. Luke's version is shorter and sharper than Matthew's, with direct 'you' address and explicit woes that balance the blessings. The reversals are total: what the world calls fortunate, Jesus calls dangerous.

Jesus teaches: love your enemies, do good to those who hate you, bless those who curse you, pray for those who mistreat you. If someone slaps you on one cheek, turn the other also. If someone takes your coat, don't withhold your shirt. Give to everyone who asks, and if someone takes what's yours, don't demand it back. Do to others as you would have them do to you. If you love only those who love you, what credit is that? Even sinners do that. If you do good only to those who are good to you, what credit is that? If you lend only to those from whom you expect repayment, what credit is that? Love your enemies, do good, and lend without expecting anything back. Your reward will be great, and you will be children of the Most High, because he is kind to the ungrateful and wicked. Be merciful, just as your Father is merciful. The standard isn't fairness — it's the character of God himself. God is generous to people who don't deserve it, and his children should be the same.

Don't judge, and you won't be judged. Don't condemn, and you won't be condemned. Forgive, and you will be forgiven. Give, and it will be given to you — a good measure, pressed down, shaken together, and running over, poured into your lap. For with the measure you use, it will be measured to you. He tells a parable: can the blind lead the blind? Won't they both fall into a pit? A student is not above the teacher, but everyone who is fully trained will be like their teacher. Why do you look at the speck of sawdust in your brother's eye and pay no attention to the plank in your own? How can you say 'brother, let me take the speck out of your eye' when you can't see the plank in your own? You hypocrite — first take the plank out of your own eye, then you'll see clearly to remove the speck from your brother's. Self-awareness comes before correction. You can't fix what you can't see in yourself.

No good tree bears bad fruit, nor does a bad tree bear good fruit. Each tree is recognised by its fruit — people don't pick figs from thornbushes or grapes from briers. A good man brings good things out of the good stored in his heart; an evil man brings evil from the evil stored in his. The mouth speaks what the heart is full of. Then Jesus asks: why do you call me Lord, Lord, and don't do what I say? Everyone who comes to him, hears his words, and puts them into practice is like a man building a house who digs deep and lays a foundation on rock. When the flood comes, it can't shake the house because it's well built. But the one who hears and doesn't act is like a man who builds a house on the ground without a foundation — the moment the torrent strikes, it collapses, and the destruction is complete. Hearing without doing is worse than not hearing at all — it creates an illusion of stability that collapses under pressure.
